title: Rounding Large Independent Sets on Expanders
speaker: Tim Hsieh
abstract: In this talk, we will present a new approach for approximating large independent sets when the input graph is a one-sided expander—that is, the uniform random walk matrix of the graph has second eigenvalue bounded away from 1. Specifically, we show a polynomial time algorithm that can find linear-sized independent sets in one-sided expanders that are almost 3-colorable or promised to contain a 0.499n-sized independent set.

proof_stages.label: Independent-set approximation landscape
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the problem by reviewing hardness and algorithmic barriers for finding large independent sets, especially in the high-completeness regime and through the connection to coloring 3-colorable graphs.

proof_stages.label: One-sided expander setting and main theorems
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting random or two-sided expander planted-coloring methods with one-sided spectral expansion, the speaker states a hardness result for almost-4-colorable expanders and the main positive results for almost-3-colorable expanders, half-minus-epsilon independent sets, and certified vertex expanders.

proof_stages.label: Approximate packing for half-minus-epsilon independent sets
proof_stages.summary: For graphs with spectral gap Omega(epsilon), the speaker proves that among any three independent sets of size half minus epsilon, some pair must intersect in more than one fourth of the vertices by combining a two-set counting inequality with edge expansion and a three-set covering contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Rounding from almost-unique independent sets
proof_stages.summary: The packing lemma is converted into an algorithmic rounding principle: two samples from a distribution or pseudo-distribution over large independent sets have large overlap, so many vertex marginals exceed one half, and thresholding those marginals yields an independent set.

proof_stages.label: Approximate packing for 3-colorings
proof_stages.summary: For 3-colorable one-sided expanders, the speaker defines agreement of colorings modulo permutation, applies expansion to the six permutation triangles in the nine-cluster product picture, and sketches the case analysis showing that either a color class is already large or two of three colorings agree on more than half the vertices.

proof_stages.label: Rounding from coloring agreement to an independent set
proof_stages.summary: The coloring-packing conclusion is rounded analogously to the independent-set case: large expected permutation agreement forces a positive fraction of vertices to have some color marginal above one half, and one such marginal class is an independent set.

proof_stages.label: Certified vertex expansion and decomposition program
proof_stages.summary: The talk extends the viewpoint to certified small-set vertex expansion and proposes vertex-expander decomposition as a route toward arbitrary graphs, emphasizing the need to remove neighbors rather than only edges so recursively found independent sets can be unioned.

examples.name: Planted k-coloring on random and two-sided expander graphs
examples.note: The speaker recalls the model obtained by sampling a random graph, partitioning vertices into k clusters, and deleting within-cluster edges, to illustrate why spectral bottom-eigenspace methods can recover planted color classes under stronger two-sided expansion assumptions.

examples.name: Extremal six-triangle weight pattern
examples.note: Inspecting the pattern with four permutation triangles of weight one half and two near zero reveals why the low-agreement, high-expansion case forces an almost-bipartite structure.
